title: Procrastinate job/event tables and LangGraph checkpoints never pruned; queue-table growth couples to janitor correctness
claim: The worker runs without a job-retention flag and no remove_old_jobs/builtin maintenance task is scheduled, so procrastinate_jobs/procrastinate_events grow unboundedly (~144 janitor jobs/day plus every materialization/teardown/rebuild/resume). Conversely _procrastinate_job_status returns None for unknown ids and None means 'never touch the row', so if an operator ever prunes the table any still-active ThreadJob referencing a pruned id becomes permanently unreconcilable — retention and reconciliation are coupled but undesigned. The reconciler's status allowlist over raw strings also lets unknown/future procrastinate statuses fall into the act branch, and the dependency is pinned lower-bound-only.

votes.evidence:
I independently re-traced every link of the chain by reading the source and the installed procrastinate 3.8.1 package.

(1) No retention configured. `config/deploy-worker.yml:16` runs `cmd: "python manage.py procrastinate worker"` with no `--delete-jobs` flag, and the `env.clear` block (lines 24-35) does not set `WORKER_DELETE_JOBS`. In the installed worker (`.venv/.../procrastinate/worker.py:59-63`), `delete_jobs` resolves to `jobs.DeleteJobCondition.NEVER` when unset. The CLI flag (`cli.py:339-346`) has no default. Procrastinate ships a builtin `remove_old_jobs` task (`builtin_tasks.py:8-40`, calls `delete_old_jobs`) but it is NOT periodic by default — it must be explicitly scheduled, and `grep` across apps/config/mcp_server found zero references to `remove_old_jobs`/`delete_old_jobs`/`builtin`. So finished jobs are kept forever (`procrastinate_finish_job` UPDATEs status rather than DELETEs when `delete_job=false`, per `00.17.00_02_delete_finished_jobs.sql`).

(2) Events couple to jobs. `schema.sql:92` defines `procrastinate_events.job_id bigint NOT NULL REFERENCES procrastinate_jobs ON DELETE CASCADE`, and multiple events are INSERTed per job lifecycle (`schema.sql:437,478,491,503`). Events are only ever removed by cascade when their job is deleted — which never happens here — so `procrastinate_events` grows with `procrastinate_jobs`.

(3) Job production volume confirmed. Exactly two `@app.periodic` tasks exist (`apps/workspaces/tasks.py:516` cron `*/30 * * * *` = 48/day; `:819` cron `*/15 * * * *` = 96/day), total 144 janitor jobs/day, matching the claim. On top of that, materialization (`mcp_server/server.py:607`, `materialization_views.py:185`), teardown (`tasks.py:544,553`), sibling rebuild (`:456`), and resume (`:393,804`) all `defer_async` more jobs. The worker is the live production worker (Kamal-style deploy manifest); periodic tasks fire via the worker process.

(4) Reconciliation coupling confirmed. `_procrastinate_job_status` (`tasks.py:693-725`) does `ProcrastinateJob.objects.filter(id=job_id).values_list("status").afirst()`, which returns None for an unknown/pruned id (empty queryset → afirst() is None) AND on exception. `reconcile_stale_thread_job` (`:742-747`) returns None ('don't touch this row this tick') when status is None. So if an operator ever prunes a still-referenced job row, any active ThreadJob pointing at it gets status=None forever → permanently unreconcilable. Retention and reconciliation are genuinely coupled and undesigned. (Mitigating note: the only state-changing prune available today is the unscheduled builtin, which deletes only jobs finished >max_hours ago — by which time the ThreadJob is normally already terminal; the stranding requires a manual/ad-hoc prune of a job still bound to an active ThreadJob.)

(5) Status allowlist over raw strings confirmed. The installed Status enum (`jobs.py:50-56`) is {todo, doing, succeeded, failed, cancelled, aborting, aborted}. The reconciler branches on `{todo,doing}` (skip), `{failed,aborted}` (fail), else fall-through to defer-resume (`tasks.py:748,776,800-816`). So `succeeded`/`cancelled`/`aborting` and any future status reach the act branch by fall-through with no explicit allowlist — literally true.

(6) Checkpoints never pruned. `apps/chat/checkpointer.py` uses `AsyncPostgresSaver` with no TTL; grep for `adelete_thread`/`delete_checkpoint`/`aclear` across the repo found zero call sites. Each resume turn (`tasks.py:1161 agent.ainvoke`) and every chat turn writes new checkpoint rows that are never deleted.

(7) Dependency pin confirmed lower-bound-only: `pyproject.toml:34` is `procrastinate[django]>=0.28` (uv.lock resolves 3.8.1).
votes.notes: Severity calibration: CONFIRMED as a real LATENT cost-perf issue, but two nuances on the sub-claims keep this from being more than cost-perf. (a) The 'unknown/future status falls into the act branch' is true, but the act branch is the *intended* happy path (defer a benign resume for a PENDING job, idempotent via CAS on PENDING/CANCELLED at tasks.py:1043-1050), not a destructive action — so robustness nit, not a correctness bug today. (b) The reconciliation-coupling stranding scenario requires an operator to manually prune procrastinate_jobs that are still bound to an *active* ThreadJob; the only built-in prune (remove_old_jobs) targets jobs finished >max_hours ago, by which time the ThreadJob is typically already terminal, so the coupling is a latent footgun rather than a live break. Both fixes are cheap and well-scoped: (i) set delete_jobs=successful or schedule remove_old_jobs periodically, (ii) tighten the reconciler to treat 'unknown job id' distinctly from 'transient error', and (iii) pin an upper bound on procrastinate. The core unbounded-growth claim is solid and reachable in production today.

title: Connection-per-operation cost: fresh TLS psycopg connection per MCP query/describe/list and per artifact query; fresh MCP client + tools/list HTTP round trip per chat request
claim: _execute_async and _execute_async_parameterized open a fresh psycopg TLS connection (sslmode require) per invocation with no pool anywhere in mcp_server, paid by every agent query/describe_table, every list_tables reconciliation, every per-table loop in get_metadata and system-prompt building, and every artifact source query (one agent turn against a 15-table schema can open 20+ serial TLS connections); artifact query-data does the same from the API process. get_mcp_tools constructs a new MultiServerMCPClient and performs a tools/list HTTP round trip on every chat message and resume though tool schemas are static, and _persist_synthetic_failure_message builds the entire agent graph (MCP call, knowledge retrieval, schema fetch, LLM bind, compile) just to append one fixed AIMessage, coupling janitor failure-messaging to MCP availability. No PgBouncer in deploy configs.

votes.evidence:
Independently re-traced every link; the connection-per-operation mechanism and reachability are real, one sub-claim's frequency is overstated.

1) Fresh TLS connection per op (no pool): mcp_server/services/query.py:40-41 `_execute_async` does `async with (await psycopg.AsyncConnection.connect(**ctx.connection_params, autocommit=True) as conn ...)`; query.py:72-73 `_execute_async_parameterized` identical. connection_params carries `sslmode` defaulting to "require" (context.py:161 `sslmode = qs.get("sslmode", ["require"])[0]`). Grep for pool/Pool/AsyncConnectionPool in mcp_server/ finds only `requests.Session` HTTP pooling in loaders — NO DB pool. Each op opens+closes its own TLS connection.

2) Per agent query/describe/list: execute_query (query.py:129) -> _execute_async. pipeline_describe_table (metadata.py:199), workspace_list_tables (metadata.py:167), and _live_tables_in_schema reconciliation (metadata.py:145) each -> _execute_async_parameterized. All exposed via live MCP agent tools list_tables/describe_table/get_metadata/query (mcp_server/server.py:108,177,232,351). Reachable today.

3) Per-table fan-out: pipeline_get_metadata loops `for t in tables_list: detail = await pipeline_describe_table(...)` (metadata.py:343-344) -> one fresh connection per table. Same loop in system-prompt schema fetch (apps/agents/graph/base.py:268-269). 15-table schema => 15+ serial TLS connects in get_metadata plus list reconciliation; "20+" is plausible.

4) Artifact source query: apps/artifacts/views.py:815-822 `for i, entry in enumerate(artifact.source_queries): ... result = await execute_query(ctx, sql)` — one connection per source query, from the API process, behind a live View.

5) get_mcp_tools per chat message: apps/agents/mcp_client.py:51-54 `client = MultiServerMCPClient({...}); tools = await client.get_tools()` builds a new client + tools/list round trip each call. Called at apps/chat/views.py:155 `mcp_tools = await get_mcp_tools()` on every POST, and again per resume via _build_agent_for_resume (apps/workspaces/tasks.py:857).

6) _persist_synthetic_failure_message builds whole graph for one AIMessage: apps/workspaces/tasks.py:911-919 calls _build_agent_for_resume (get_mcp_tools + build_agent_graph) then `agent.aupdate_state(config, {"messages": [AIMessage(content=text)]})`. Reachable from live periodic janitor: apps/workspaces/tasks.py:819 `@app.periodic(cron="*/15 * * * *")` expire_stale_thread_jobs -> reconcile_stale_thread_job -> _persist_synthetic_failure_message (tasks.py:774, 798). Couples janitor messaging to MCP availability — confirmed.

7) No PgBouncer: no pgbouncer/pool in docker-compose.yml, docker-compose.override.yml, or infra/scout-stack.yml.

CORRECTION (why PARTIAL not CONFIRMED): the finding bundles the per-table schema fan-out under "system-prompt building" implying it is paid every chat message. _build_system_prompt is behind a 60s TTL cache (apps/agents/graph/base.py:127-128 `_system_prompt_cache`, `_SYSTEM_PROMPT_TTL = 60`; checked at 717-722, stored at 774). So the N-connection schema fan-out in the prompt path is amortized within a 60s window, not per message. The get_metadata MCP tool itself is uncached and still pays full N per invocation, and cold-cache / >60s-idle chats pay it too — so the defect is real, only its per-message framing for the prompt path is overstated.
votes.notes: All file:line references in the finding are accurate. Mechanism, reachability (live agent tools, live artifact View, every chat POST, and the */15 periodic janitor), and "no PgBouncer" all independently confirmed. Only correction: the schema per-table fan-out in the system-prompt path is throttled by a 60s prompt cache, so it is not literally paid on "every chat message" as the prose suggests — but the uncached get_metadata MCP tool, cold caches, and >60s-idle turns still incur it, and every execute_query/describe/list/artifact-query and every get_mcp_tools call are genuinely per-invocation with no caching. Severity DEBT / impact cost-perf are appropriate; the prompt-cache nuance trims the magnitude of one sub-path, not the finding.

title: Data dictionary view: per-table TableKnowledge N+1, two fresh managed-DB connections per request, and async_to_sync in a sync DRF view
claim: DataDictionaryView._get_from_pipeline calls _get_annotation inside the per-table loop, issuing one TableKnowledge.objects.get per table (N+1; one filter would do); the same request opens two separate fresh managed-DB connections (_get_all_columns and _live_tables_in_schema via pipeline_list_tables) and runs async_to_sync inside a sync DRF view (new event loop per request).

votes.evidence:
I independently re-traced all three sub-claims plus reachability.

1. N+1 TableKnowledge query — CONFIRMED. apps/workspaces/api/views.py:287 `for table_info in tables_list:` then line 290 `annotation = _get_annotation(workspace, qualified_name)` inside the loop. `_get_annotation` (views.py:216-222) runs `tk = TableKnowledge.objects.get(workspace=workspace, table_name=table_name)` — one query per table. A single `filter(workspace=..., table_name__in=[...])` pre-fetch would collapse this to one query.

2. Two fresh managed-DB connections per request — CONFIRMED.
   - views.py:282 `all_columns = _get_all_columns(schema_name)` → views.py:80 `conn = get_managed_db_connection()` → schema_manager.py:38-43 returns `psycopg.connect(url, autocommit=True)` (fresh sync conn, closed at views.py:93).
   - views.py:275 `async_to_sync(pipeline_list_tables)(tenant_schema, pipeline_config)` → metadata.py:76 `live_table_names = await _live_tables_in_schema(tenant_schema.schema_name)` → metadata.py:115/145 `_execute_async_parameterized(...)` → query.py:72-73 `async with (await psycopg.AsyncConnection.connect(**ctx.connection_params, autocommit=True) as conn ...)` (fresh async conn). Two distinct, unpooled connections.

3. async_to_sync in a sync DRF view — CONFIRMED. views.py:225 `class DataDictionaryView(APIView)`, views.py:236 `def get(self, request, workspace_id)` (sync), and views.py:275 wraps the coroutine in `async_to_sync(...)`, spinning a new event loop per request.

Reachability — CONFIRMED LIVE. Route at apps/workspaces/api/urls.py:16 `path("data-dictionary/", DataDictionaryView.as_view(), ...)`. Frontend fetches `/api/workspaces/${activeDomainId}/data-dictionary/` at frontend/src/store/dictionarySlice.ts:178 and :200; the page is reachable via Sidebar.tsx:95 nav item, router.tsx:37, and EmbedPage.tsx:41.
votes.notes: All three mechanisms and the file:line citations are accurate. Severity classification (DEBT / cost-perf) is appropriate: this is a perf inefficiency on a user-facing but non-streaming, on-demand GET, not a correctness or data-loss bug — connections are properly closed (try/finally at views.py:92-93; async-with context manager at query.py:72) so there is no leak, just per-request overhead. The N+1 magnitude scales with table count (one extra TableKnowledge.get per surfaced table). The two-connections point is slightly understated as "two": _get_all_columns and _live_tables_in_schema each open one, but note _get_all_columns and pipeline_list_tables both independently query information_schema for the same schema (columns vs tables) and could in principle be unified. No refutation found.

title: CI never runs the 2026-06-10 incident regression tests or any real-DB writer/role-isolation tests (module-level MANAGED_DATABASE_URL skip)
claim: tests/test_view_schema_builder.py, test_ocs_materializer.py, and the TestConnectIdlessWriters / CommCare writer tests in test_materializer.py carry module-level pytestmark skipif(not MANAGED_DATABASE_URL/DATABASE_URL); no GitHub workflow sets those vars (test.py documents CI sets neither), so all of them skip — including the #227 truncation-collision regressions, the four fully-mocked #229 last_error-surfacing regressions that need no DB, the f26c1a0 duplication and 2587158 missing-id regressions, and the role-isolation grant tests. tenant isolation (SET ROLE) is only tested against a MagicMock asserting 'SET ROLE' strings. The regressions written for the production incidents have never executed in CI; locally they fall back to DATABASE_URL and run DROP SCHEMA CASCADE against the dev DB.

votes.evidence: Independently reproduced. (1) CI sets neither var: .github/workflows/ci.yml test job env block contains only DATABASE_USER/DATABASE_PASSWORD/TEST_DATABASE_NAME/DATABASE_HOST/DATABASE_PORT/DJANGO_SECRET_KEY/DJANGO_SETTINGS_MODULE; `grep -rn "MANAGED_DATABASE_URL\|DATABASE_URL" .github/workflows/*.yml` returns zero matches across all 5 workflows. config/settings/test.py:17 comment confirms: "CI: sets DATABASE_USER/PASSWORD/HOST/PORT explicitly (no DATABASE_URL)". `.env` is gitignored (git check-ignore confirms, not in git ls-files), so config/settings/base.py:26 `env.read_env(str(env_file))` injects nothing in CI. (2) Module-level skips verified by reading: tests/test_view_schema_builder.py:19-22 `pytestmark = pytest.mark.skipif(not os.environ.get("MANAGED_DATABASE_URL"), ...)`; tests/test_ocs_materializer.py:23-26 identical. tests/test_materializer.py uses per-test skips with DATABASE_URL fallback: lines 1090-1092 `db_url = os.environ.get("MANAGED_DATABASE_URL") or os.environ.get("DATABASE_URL"); if not db_url: pytest.skip(...)` (also 1143, 1211, 1292, 1361). (3) Empirical reproduction: hid .env and ran pytest with exactly the CI env vars — result "31 passed, 23 skipped": ALL 13 test_view_schema_builder tests skipped (incl. #227 truncation regressions at 448/527/678 and the 4 fully-mocked no-DB tests at 353/394/724/760, the latter two being the #229 last_error producer regressions `test_build_view_schema_clears_last_error_on_success` and `test_build_view_schema_records_last_error_on_failure`), ALL 5 test_ocs_materializer tests skipped, ALL 5 real-DB writer tests in test_materializer skipped (incl. TestConnectPageReplayIdempotency — the f26c1a0 duplication / 2587158 missing-id regressions; both commits verified to exist). (4) Role isolation: tests/test_query_role_isolation.py has no skipif and runs in CI, but TestSetRoleIsolation (line ~40) patches `psycopg.AsyncConnection.connect` with AsyncMock and asserts `"SET ROLE" in str(execute_calls[0])` — pure string assertion on a mock. The only real-grant test (test_build_view_schema_readonly_role_has_access, test_view_schema_builder.py:292) is under the skipped module. (5) Local dev-DB exposure: .env has DATABASE_URL and MANAGED_DATABASE_URL both pointing at postgresql://platform:***@localhost:5432/agent_platform (the dev platform DB, not the Django test DB); with .env present all 18 module-skip tests ran against it, and the writer tests psycopg.connect() to that DB and execute `DROP SCHEMA IF EXISTS test_write_cases/test_cpr_* CASCADE` (test_materializer.py:1126, 1167).
votes.notes: Core claim fully reproduced; minor corrections: (a) the test_materializer.py class is TestConnectPageReplayIdempotency (line 1173), not "TestConnectIdlessWriters", and it uses per-test pytest.skip with a DATABASE_URL fallback rather than a module-level pytestmark — same CI effect (skipped). (b) Of the four fully-mocked skipped builder tests, two are #229 last_error-surfacing regressions (725, 761); the other two (354, 395) are bulk-fetch/active-record regressions — still needlessly skipped by the module mark. (c) "#229 regressions never executed in CI" is true for the producer side (build_view_schema writing/clearing last_error) but the consumer-side surfacing tests DO run in CI unskipped (test_mcp_tenant_tools.py:658, test_resume_thread_task.py:962 assert on last_error with no skipif), so #229 has partial CI coverage. (d) The local DROP SCHEMA CASCADE risk is scoped to fixed test-named schemas (test_write_cases, test_cpr_payments, etc.) in the dev DB — real pollution/clobber risk but not arbitrary-schema destruction. Status BROKEN-NOW and impact correctness are appropriate: the CI gate today silently passes while every real-DB regression for the 2026-06-10 incidents is skipped; reachability is total (every PR to main runs this workflow).

votes.evidence:
Independently re-traced, including against a real CI run log (run 27346157427, 2026-06-11, branch bdr/sibling-view-schema-consistency).

CONFIRMED elements:
1. Module-level skips exist exactly as claimed. /Users/bderenzi/Code/dimagi/scout/tests/test_view_schema_builder.py:19-22 — `pytestmark = pytest.mark.skipif(not os.environ.get("MANAGED_DATABASE_URL"), reason="MANAGED_DATABASE_URL not set")`. Same pattern at tests/test_ocs_materializer.py:23-26. tests/test_materializer.py writer tests use runtime skips: line 1090/1141/1190 `db_url = os.environ.get("MANAGED_DATABASE_URL") or os.environ.get("DATABASE_URL")` then `pytest.skip(...)` (lines 1092, 1143, 1211, 1292, 1361).
2. CI sets neither var. .github/workflows/ci.yml test job env block sets only DATABASE_USER/PASSWORD/HOST/PORT, TEST_DATABASE_NAME, DJANGO_SECRET_KEY, DJANGO_SETTINGS_MODULE — no DATABASE_URL, no MANAGED_DATABASE_URL anywhere in any workflow (grepped all of .github/workflows/). config/settings/test.py:16-17 documents this: "CI: sets DATABASE_USER/PASSWORD/HOST/PORT explicitly (no DATABASE_URL)". .env is gitignored and not committed.
3. Direct CI-log proof: in run 27346157427 ALL 12 tests in test_view_schema_builder.py are SKIPPED — including the #227 truncation regressions (`test_build_view_schema_long_canonical_name_no_truncation_collision`, `test_build_view_schema_two_long_names_shared_head_get_distinct_prefixes`, file lines 448/527), the real-DB role grant test (`test_build_view_schema_readonly_role_has_access`, line 292), and the fully-mocked tests that need no managed DB (`test_build_view_schema_bulk_fetches_tenant_schemas` line 354, `test_build_view_schema_returns_active_record` line 395, `test_build_view_schema_clears_last_error_on_success` line 725, `test_build_view_schema_records_last_error_on_failure` line 761 — all use MagicMock + patch of get_managed_db_connection). All 5 test_ocs_materializer.py tests SKIPPED. TestConnectPageReplayIdempotency idless tests (the 2587158 regressions, reworking the f26c1a0 ones) SKIPPED.
4. SET ROLE isolation is mock-only in CI: tests/test_query_role_isolation.py:48-70 patches `psycopg.AsyncConnection.connect` with AsyncMock and asserts `"SET ROLE" in str(execute_calls[0])` — string assertions on a MagicMock; these PASSED in CI. The only real-DB grant/role test is in the skipped module.
5. Local destructive behavior: test_materializer.py writer tests do fall back to DATABASE_URL and run `DROP SCHEMA IF EXISTS {test_schema} CASCADE` (e.g. lines ~1128, 1196) via a direct psycopg connection to that URL — i.e., against the live local platform/dev DB, not the pytest-created scout_test DB.

REFUTED/CORRECTED elements:
A. "The regressions written for the production incidents have never executed in CI" is overstated. #229 (ef42b4e) added regression tests in FOUR files; three of them have no skip and run in CI: tests/test_resume_thread_task.py (`test_resume_surfaces_view_schema_failure_for_multi_tenant`, `test_resume_plain_completed_for_multi_tenant_active_view_schema` — PASSED in run 27346157427), tests/test_mcp_tenant_tools.py (`TestGetSchemaStatusTool::test_returns_failed_with_error_for_failed_view_schema` — PASSED), and tests/test_workspace_last_synced.py (derive_schema_status tests — PASSED). So the #229 surfacing chain (resume task, MCP status, status API) IS exercised in CI; only the SchemaManager last_error persistence layer (the two mocked tests in the skipped module) is not.
B. "the four fully-mocked #229 last_error-surfacing regressions" misattributes: test_view_schema_builder.py contains four fully-mocked tests, but only two are #229 last_error regressions (lines 725, 761); the other two mocked tests (lines 354, 395) predate #229 (truncation-fix commit 2aaf4fb / earlier). All four are nonetheless pointlessly skipped by the module-level mark.
C. Local-fallback nuance: test_view_schema_builder.py and test_ocs_materializer.py have NO DATABASE_URL fallback — they require MANAGED_DATABASE_URL, which locally comes from .env (django-environ read_env populates os.environ; both DATABASE_URL and MANAGED_DATABASE_URL are present in the local .env). Only test_materializer.py writer tests fall back to DATABASE_URL. Either way they execute DDL incl. DROP SCHEMA CASCADE against a live local DB (fixed test_*/build_domain_* schema names, so blast radius is bounded).
votes.notes: Core finding stands and is proven from an actual CI run log, not just code reading: every test in test_view_schema_builder.py (incl. #227 truncation-collision regressions and the real-DB readonly-role grant test), all of test_ocs_materializer.py, and the Connect idless-writer regressions (f26c1a0/2587158) skip silently in CI today, and SET ROLE isolation is only string-asserted against mocks in CI. BROKEN-NOW/correctness is the right rating for the CI coverage gap. PARTIAL rather than CONFIRMED because the claim's strongest sentence — "the regressions written for the production incidents have never executed in CI" — is false for #229: its end-to-end surfacing regressions in test_resume_thread_task.py, test_mcp_tenant_tools.py, and test_workspace_last_synced.py run and pass in CI; only the two mocked SchemaManager last_error tests are trapped behind the module-level skip. Cheap fix available: move the four fully-mocked tests out from under the module pytestmark (or scope the mark to real-DB tests), and have CI export MANAGED_DATABASE_URL pointing at the existing postgres service so the real-DB suites run.

votes.evidence:

TestConnectPageReplayIdempotency at 1199/1281/1351 — the f26c1a0/2587158 idless/page-replay regressions; both commits verified). (4) Role isolation in CI is mock-only: tests/test_query_role_isolation.py patches `psycopg.AsyncConnection.connect` with AsyncMock (lines 57-58, 86-87) and string-asserts `"SET ROLE" in first_call_str` (line 66); these 5 tests passed under CI env; the only real-grant test (line 292) is in the skipped module. (5) Local exposure: .env contains both DATABASE_URL and MANAGED_DATABASE_URL pointing at postgresql://platform:***@localhost:5432/agent_platform (the live dev platform DB, not the pytest scout_test DB); writer tests psycopg.connect() to it and run `DROP SCHEMA IF EXISTS {test_schema} CASCADE` (test_materializer.py:1128, 1168, 1195, 1278, 1348, 1423) — fixed test-named schemas, bounded blast radius.

REFUTED/CORRECTED elements: (A) "The regressions written for the production incidents have never executed in CI" is false as stated, for BOTH #227 and #229. #227 (2aaf4fb) also added tests/test_schema_manager.py with NO skipif — TestViewPrefix unit tests (short/boundary/hashed/deterministic/shared-head-distinct-prefixes) exercise the truncation-collision prefix logic and run in CI; neither prior verifier surfaced this. #229 (ef42b4e) added regressions in four files; three run unskipped in CI: tests/test_resume_thread_task.py:952 (test_resume_surfaces_view_schema_failure_for_multi_tenant), tests/test_mcp_tenant_tools.py:640 (TestGetSchemaStatusTool::test_returns_failed_with_error_for_failed_view_schema — the MANAGED_DATABASE_URL grep hits there are override_settings, not skips), and tests/test_workspace_last_synced.py. Only the integration/real-DB layer (builder end-to-end at 448/527/678, the two mocked SchemaManager last_error tests at 725/761, the writer idempotency tests) skips. 2587158's resumability-flag tests in tests/test_pipeline_registry.py also run in CI (no skipif). (B) Class name wrong: no "TestConnectIdlessWriters" exists; it is TestConnectPageReplayIdempotency (test_materializer.py:1173), and test_materializer.py uses per-test pytest.skip, not a module-level pytestmark — same CI effect. (C) The four fully-mocked skipped tests are not all "#229 last_error-surfacing regressions": only 725/761 are; 353/394 (bulk-fetch, active-record) predate #229 — all four are still needlessly trapped by the module mark.
votes.notes: Settling the CONFIRMED-vs-PARTIAL split in favor of PARTIAL, per the verdict rules (severity/mechanism misstated even though the defect is real). The CI gap itself is real, reproduced byte-for-byte (23 silent skips under exact CI env), and BROKEN-NOW/correctness is right: every real-DB integration test — truncation-collision end-to-end, readonly-role grants, OCS writers, Connect page-replay/idless writers — silently skips on every PR, and SET ROLE isolation is only string-asserted against mocks. But the finding's headline causal claim ("the regressions written for the production incidents have never executed in CI") is wrong for both #227 and #229: each has unit/consumer-level regressions that run and pass in CI (test_schema_manager.py TestViewPrefix for #227 — a fact even verifier #1 missed; test_resume_thread_task/test_mcp_tenant_tools/test_workspace_last_synced for #229; test_pipeline_registry for 2587158). What CI lacks is the real-DB integration layer, which is a materially smaller (though still serious) hole than "zero incident-regression coverage". Also misnames the test class and the skip mechanism in test_materializer.py. Cheap fixes: scope the module pytestmarks to the real-DB tests (freeing the 4 fully-mocked tests at 353/394/725/761), and export MANAGED_DATABASE_URL in ci.yml pointing at the existing postgres:16 service so the integration suites actually run.

title: Chat-to-MCP wire has zero unmocked coverage; tests mock the exact seams that hid production bugs
claim: No test starts the FastMCP server and connects the real mcp_client to it: test_mcp_client patches MultiServerMCPClient, test_mcp_chat_integration mocks get_mcp_tools with hand-written fakes whose schemas can drift from server.py, and test_mcp_server/test_mcp_tenant_tools call handlers in-process with the DB mocked at the ORM/psycopg boundary. The highest-churn, highest-fix-density seam (server.py, graph/base.py, chat/views.py) is the one seam the suite cannot observe — the same masking pattern that hid the recipes signature drift (build_agent_graph patched), the refresh data-loss (run_pipeline patched), and a prior SynchronousOnlyOperation bug (aresolve_credential patched). MCP_TOOL_NAMES membership is a cross-process contract enforced only by hand, and its test asserts the constant against itself; the dbt-execution path has zero non-mocked coverage.

votes.evidence:
I independently re-traced every sub-claim by reading the files, not trusting the finding's quotes.

1) "No test starts FastMCP server + connects real mcp_client": CONFIRMED. `grep MultiServerMCPClient tests/` returns ONLY tests/test_mcp_client.py, which patches it: `with patch("apps.agents.mcp_client.MultiServerMCPClient", return_value=mock_client)` (test_mcp_client.py:30, also 52, 70). No test uses an HTTP/streamable/ASGI transport against mcp_server/server.py; `grep "mcp.run|streamable_http|http_app|ASGITransport"` across tests/ yields nothing pointing at the MCP server (only run_materialization/list_pipelines in-process asyncio.run calls).

2) "test_mcp_chat_integration mocks get_mcp_tools with hand-written fakes": CONFIRMED. The view seam apps.chat.views.get_mcp_tools is patched (test_mcp_chat_integration.py:247,290,315,692,734,798,841,869) and fakes are hand-built (`mock_tool = MagicMock(); mock_tool.name = "query"` at lines 311-313, 362-363, 392-396). Schemas are authored in-test, decoupled from server.py's @mcp.tool() definitions (server.py:107,176,231,289,333,378,407,445,520,651,801).

3) "Masking pattern that hid recipes drift / refresh data-loss / SynchronousOnlyOperation": CONFIRMED that the suite patches the very seams. build_agent_graph is patched (test_mcp_chat_integration.py:249,317,694,736,800,843,871). run_materialization is mocked in test_mcp_tenant_tools.py and others; run_pipeline appears in tests/test_refresh_task.py mock list.

4) "MCP_TOOL_NAMES test asserts the constant against itself": CONFIRMED. test_agent_graph.py:9-22 imports MCP_TOOL_NAMES (defined as a frozenset at graph/base.py:65-76) and asserts string literals are `in` it — tautological; no cross-process check that server.py registers exactly those tools.

5) "dbt-execution path has zero non-mocked coverage": CONFIRMED. All run_dbt tests patch dbtRunner: `patch("mcp_server.services.dbt_runner.dbtRunner", ...)` (test_dbt_runner.py:63,85,105,138). No real dbt invocation anywhere.

6) Reachability: CONFIRMED live. apps.chat.views (route /api/chat/) calls get_mcp_tools and build_agent_graph in production; run_materialization tool (server.py:521) defers the real materialize_workspace procrastinate task. The untested wire IS the live wire.

One nuance requiring correction below: the finding's blanket statement that the server tests mock "at the ORM/psycopg boundary" omits that test_mcp_tenant_tools.py:853-989 (TestExecuteAsyncIntegration) runs _execute_async/_execute_async_parameterized against a REAL PostgreSQL (gated on DATABASE_URL env). So the psycopg execution layer is NOT entirely unmocked. This does not weaken the central thesis (no FastMCP-wire/real-mcp_client coverage, dbt fully mocked, tautological constant test) — all hold.
votes.notes: Verdict CONFIRMED on the central thesis with a minor accuracy correction. The finding overstates one detail: it implies ALL server-tool DB access is mocked at the ORM/psycopg boundary, but test_mcp_tenant_tools.py::TestExecuteAsyncIntegration (lines 853-989) exercises _execute_async and _execute_async_parameterized against a live PostgreSQL when DATABASE_URL is set, including a real regression test for the psycopg3 `SET statement_timeout TO $1` syntax error. So the query-execution layer has SOME unmocked coverage; the gap is specifically the FastMCP tool-registration/transport wire and the agent<->MCP-client handshake (MultiServerMCPClient -> get_tools -> tool schema -> bind_tools -> injection), which is never exercised end to end. Severity/status (DEBT, correctness) is appropriate and unchanged: this is a genuine coverage gap on the highest-churn seam, not a live break. The MCP_TOOL_NAMES tautology and dbt-mock claims are fully accurate. No exaggeration material enough to downgrade to PARTIAL — the one inaccuracy is peripheral and does not alter the conclusion that the chat-to-MCP wire has no unmocked coverage.

title: Frontend has no unit-test infrastructure; e2e runs nowhere; post-deploy smoke tests exist but are wired to nothing
claim: frontend/package.json has no test runner — only Playwright e2e scripts that never run in CI (the frontend job is ESLint only) and partly require live credentials or interactive mode; the threadId-leak incident fix (00c423d, useWorkspaceThreadSync.ts) and workspace switching have no regression coverage, and 13k LOC of hand-written TS API-shape mirrors are verified only by tsc against themselves (the same disease that produced the onboarding 404 and phantom is_public types). tests/smoke/test_deployment.py and test_connect_sync.py exist but are excluded by default addopts ('-m not smoke') and no deploy workflow runs any pytest/playwright step — both June incidents were detected by humans noticing stuck UI, while an automated post-deploy smoke run is already written and never invoked.

votes.evidence:
Re-traced every link independently.

1. No frontend unit-test runner: frontend/package.json devDependencies = eslint/playwright/vite/typescript tooling only; scripts are "dev/build/lint/preview/test:e2e/test:e2e:widget/test:e2e:integration". grep of package.json+bun.lock for vitest|jest|@testing-library|mocha|ava found no test runner (only an unrelated radix-ui dep substring). `find src -name '*.test.*' -o -name '*.spec.*'` returns nothing.

2. CI frontend job is ESLint-only: .github/workflows/ci.yml lines 23-36 `lint-frontend` runs `bun run lint` (line 35). Backend `test` job line 68 runs `uv run pytest`. grep -E 'pytest|playwright|smoke|test:e2e' across all workflows: only ci.yml:68 (`uv run pytest`) and claude.yml:93 (a string inside an `--allowedTools` permission list, not a run step). No playwright/test:e2e invocation in any workflow.

3. e2e runs nowhere + live creds/interactive: playwright.config.ts defines projects widget-sdk, embed-integration, connect-tenant, labs-smoke. labs-smoke.spec.ts hardcodes `CONNECT_EMAIL = "jjackson+test@dimagi.com"` / `CONNECT_PASSWORD = "Uganda123"` and launches `chromium.launchPersistentContext(... headless: false ...)` — interactive, live-OAuth. config also sets labs-smoke `headless: false`. Nothing runs `test:e2e`/playwright in CI or deploy.

4. threadId-leak fix uncovered: `git show 00c423d` = "fix(chat): stop carrying threads across workspaces and recover from stale threads" (Brian DeRenzi, 2026-06-10). frontend/src/hooks/useWorkspaceThreadSync.ts exists. grep for useWorkspaceThreadSync/WorkspaceThreadSync in tests → "(no test references the hook)".

5. ~13k LOC hand-written TS, tsc-only: `find src -name '*.ts' -o -name '*.tsx'` (excluding test/spec) = 13085 total LOC; 133 interface/type declarations; no openapi/orval/*.schema.json codegen present. is_public types hand-written in store/recipeSlice.ts:44, store/uiSlice.ts:11,19. build = `tsc -b && vite build` (only static check).

6. smoke wired to nothing: pyproject.toml:97 `addopts = "-v --tb=short -m 'not smoke'"` excludes smoke by default. tests/smoke/test_deployment.py classes all `@pytest.mark.smoke`; tests/smoke/test_connect_sync.py:121 `@pytest.mark.smoke`. deploy.yml and deploy-labs.yml contain zero pytest/playwright/smoke steps (only docker build/push + kamal/ecs deploy + service-stable waits). So the written post-deploy smoke suite is invoked by nothing.

Reachability: all paths are live today — ci.yml triggers on every PR to main (frontend job ESLint-only); deploy.yml triggers on every push to main and deploy-labs.yml on workflow_dispatch (neither runs tests); the threadsync hook is shipped production code (referenced from chat routing per commit). The smoke suites are dead in the sense the finding claims (never invoked by automation).
votes.notes: One rhetorical imprecision, not material: "13k LOC of hand-written TS API-shape mirrors" — 13,085 is the TOTAL non-test src/ TS/TSX LOC (components, hooks, pages, utils), not solely API-shape mirrors. The genuinely hand-mirrored surface is smaller (src/api = 388 LOC, src/store = 1034 LOC, 133 interface/type decls). The substantive point is fully correct: there is no type codegen from the backend, so all request/response shapes are hand-written and checked only by tsc against themselves, which is exactly the failure mode behind the is_public / onboarding-404 type drift. Status DEBT and impact velocity are accurate: this is missing/unwired test infrastructure, not a runtime defect — nothing is broken-now, but two June production incidents (threadId leak, stuck UI) were human-detected despite an already-written smoke suite that no workflow runs. Recommend keeping the finding as-is but softening the "13k LOC API-shape mirrors" phrasing to "~13k LOC of hand-written TS (no codegen), with request/response shapes mirrored by hand and verified only by tsc."

title: Cosmetic drift cluster: stale Celery docstrings, removed-model prompt sections, doubled Knowledge Base heading, inline imports, three-strata naming residue
claim: RefreshSchemaView/create_refresh_schema docstrings still say 'Celery task' 13 months after the Procrastinate migration; BASE_SYSTEM_PROMPT carries a CRITICAL 'Canonical Metrics' section for a model replaced by generic KnowledgeEntry; _build_system_prompt wraps retriever output in '## Knowledge Base' while the retriever emits its own, doubling the heading in every prompt with entries; DataDictionaryView's docstring states the wrong route/'latest completed' (also accepts PARTIAL); inline imports at base.py:244,261 violate the project import policy (27897fc sweep missed them); TODO.md lists SET ROLE isolation as unimplemented though query.py implements it; assorted false docstrings (user_role 'controls access', setCachedUserTenants cache invariant, mcp auth token injection, question-blind retriever, completed_modules INTEGER-vs-TEXT, _RESUMABLE_CONNECT_SOURCES comment, _load_and_commit_source atomicity); _json_safe Decimal-to-float coercion is dead; CommCareCaseLoader legacy access_token kwarg kept alive only by its own tests; DEPLOYMENT.md drift inventory (Redis 'Celery broker', nginx '/mcp/' location, scout/mcp ECR image, pre-deploy hook all stale; pre-connect hook contradicts the documented SSH setup); frontend store still speaks 'domain' vs API 'workspace' vs UI 'data source'; the frontend-layout rule contradicts the pages it cites as exemplars.

votes.evidence:
I independently re-traced the cluster. The vast majority of sub-claims are CONFIRMED, but two facts in the finding are wrong/imprecise (timeline + a nonexistent hook), so PARTIAL.

CONFIRMED sub-claims (re-traced myself):
- Stale Celery docstrings: apps/workspaces/api/views.py:319 "dispatches a Celery task" and apps/workspaces/services/schema_manager.py:174 "Celery task (refresh_tenant_schema)". grep for "celery" across *.py/*.toml returns ONLY these two docstrings. The real queue is Procrastinate: pyproject.toml:34 "procrastinate[django]>=0.28"; Procfile worker runs `manage.py procrastinate worker`. Both RefreshSchemaView and create_refresh_schema are live (RefreshSchemaView wired at apps/workspaces/api/urls.py:22 `refresh/`; create_refresh_schema feeds the materialization path). CONFIRMED.
- Canonical Metrics CRITICAL section: base_system.py:81-94 has "## Canonical Metrics (CRITICAL)" with MUST-use language; the retriever (apps/knowledge/services/retriever.py) only emits generic KnowledgeEntry/TableKnowledge/AgentLearning sections — no canonical-metric model. base_system.py:8-12 module docstring still references "Canonical metrics and their SQL definitions". CONFIRMED.
- Doubled "## Knowledge Base" heading: base.py:733 `sections.append(f"\n## Knowledge Base\n\n{knowledge_context}\n")` AND retriever.py:58 `lines: list[str] = ["## Knowledge Base", ""]`. Retriever output is fed verbatim into the f-string, so any workspace with KnowledgeEntry rows gets the heading twice. This is the LIVE prompt-build path (_build_system_prompt at base.py:700). CONFIRMED.
- Inline runtime imports: base.py:244 `from apps.transformations.services.lineage import aget_terminal_assets` and base.py:261 `from apps.workspaces.models import TenantMetadata`, both inside function bodies. (Contrast: base.py:56-60 imports are under `if TYPE_CHECKING:` — legitimately allowed.) These violate the documented import policy (CLAUDE.md). The 27897fc sweep (2026-04-16 "move inline imports to module level") did NOT touch base.py (empty `--stat` grep). CONFIRMED present today.
- DataDictionaryView docstring: views.py:226-231 says `GET /api/data-dictionary/` and "latest completed MaterializationRun". Actual route is nested: apps/workspaces/api/urls.py:16 `data-dictionary/` under config/urls.py:66 `/api/workspaces/<workspace_id>/`, so real route is /api/workspaces/<id>/data-dictionary/ — docstring route is wrong. And _get_from_pipeline (views.py:256-259) filters state__in=[COMPLETED, PARTIAL], so "latest completed" omits PARTIAL. Finding explicitly accepted PARTIAL here. CONFIRMED.
- TODO.md SET ROLE drift: TODO.md "PostgreSQL role isolation ... use `SET ROLE` at query time" is listed `[ ]` (unchecked, under ## Security), yet mcp_server/services/query.py:44 `await cursor.execute(psql.SQL("SET ROLE {}").format(...ctx.readonly_role))` with RESET ROLE at :65 — fully implemented in the live MCP query-execution path. CONFIRMED.
- Dead _json_safe: apps/artifacts/views.py:749 defines `_json_safe` (with Decimal->float branch at :753). Repo-wide grep for `_json_safe` finds ZERO call sites — only the definition. So the whole function is dead, not just the Decimal branch (finding understates it). CONFIRMED (as dead code).
- Legacy access_token kwarg: mcp_server/loaders/commcare_cases.py:23-34 CommCareCaseLoader.__init__ keeps `access_token` kwarg "for backwards compatibility". Only production instantiation is materializer.py:761 `CommCareCaseLoader(domain=domain, credential=credential)` — never passes access_token. Only callers using access_token= are tests (test_commcare_loader.py:23,49). CONFIRMED.
- DEPLOYMENT.md drift: line 10 "ElastiCache Redis 7 — caching and Celery broker"; line 23 "Worker (Celery) | deploy-worker.yml"; line 11 ECR "scout/mcp"; line 26 nginx proxies "/mcp/". Worker is Procrastinate (Procfile), Celery was removed in commit 7333b3d. Also no `deploy-worker.yml` exists in .github/workflows. CONFIRMED as stale.
- Frontend naming residue: frontend/src/store/domainSlice.ts exists ("domain") while backend app is `apps/workspaces` (62d329e renamed projects->workspaces) and UI says "data source". CONFIRMED.
- frontend-layout.md self-contradiction: .claude/rules/frontend-layout.md says the "Correct pattern (used by Knowledge, Recipes, Connections, etc.)" is `<div className="p-6">` and to NOT use `mx-auto` + `max-w-*`. But RecipesPage.tsx:154,169,214 use `<div className="container mx-auto px-8 py-8">` and KnowledgePage.tsx:139 uses `container mx-auto px-8 py-8` — the exact forbidden pattern. Only ConnectionsPage.tsx:176 uses the prescribed `p-6`. So two of three cited exemplars contradict the rule. CONFIRMED.

WRONG / IMPRECISE in the finding (basis for PARTIAL):
- "13 months after the Procrastinate migration" is FALSE. The entire repo's first commit is 2026-02-05 (4 months old); the Celery->Procrastinate dependency swap is commit 7333b3d dated 2026-05-01. Relative to today (2026-06-12) the stale docstrings are ~6 weeks old, not 13 months — the figure is off by ~10x. The Celery docstrings themselves were written 2026-03-17 (62d329e) when Celery was still the intended queue, so they were correct-at-authoring, now stale.
- "pre-connect hook contradicts the documented SSH setup": no `pre-connect` hook exists anywhere (only .kamal/hooks/pre-deploy). This specific sub-claim is unsupported.
- "_json_safe Decimal-to-float coercion is dead": technically true but understated — the entire function has zero callers, not just the Decimal branch.
- I could not independently confirm a few minor docstring sub-claims (user_role "controls access", setCachedUserTenants cache invariant, completed_modules INTEGER-vs-TEXT, mcp auth token injection, _load_and_commit_source atomicity) in the time budget — grep for the named anchors didn't surface them at the quoted form, so they are unverified rather than refuted.
votes.notes: Net: this is a real, dense cosmetic/docs-drift cluster. Status COSMETIC and impact velocity are correct — every confirmed item is documentation/dead-code/naming drift with no runtime behavior change (the doubled "## Knowledge Base" heading is the only one touching live output, and it's harmless markdown). Reachability holds for the code-path items (refresh views, MCP query SET ROLE, prompt builder, materializer loader all live). The finding's credibility is dinged by the "13 months" fabrication and the nonexistent "pre-connect hook", which is why this is PARTIAL not CONFIRMED, but the corrected status/impact are unchanged.

title: Minor type lies and cosmetic seam drift: TS contracts stricter than runtime payloads, per-process prompt-cache key omits schema/knowledge state, list-repr in recipe results
claim: Several hand-written TS contracts are stricter than the runtime payload (Recipe[] list items lack required prompt/variables; ArtifactPanel QueryResult.sql required but error entries carry only {name,error}; workspaceApi.create omits schema_status/last_synced_at required by WorkspaceListItem; created_by_name returned but never rendered) — none break today but each traps the next consumer. The system-prompt cache key omits schema/knowledge state and the cache is per-process, so a freshly-materialized workspace can serve a 'No data loaded yet' prompt for up to 60s in the API process; runner._extract_response_content does str(msg.content) producing a Python list-repr for content blocks; error-correlation is inconsistent (one endpoint mints refs, the stream path none, no cross-process correlation id); get_schema_status carries a third legacy {table,rows_loaded} spelling; live SSE empty tool-input vs reload real args. A large one-sided dead-surface inventory (refresh/status, transformations frontend, tenants/select, theme param, providers connected boolean) also exists.

votes.evidence:
I independently re-traced every concrete sub-claim; all check out against the actual code.

1. Recipe[] list contract stricter than payload (LIVE ROUTE). frontend/src/store/recipeSlice.ts:13-24 declares `Recipe` with `prompt: string` and `variables: RecipeVariable[]` as REQUIRED (no `?`). fetchRecipes (recipeSlice.ts:86) calls `GET /api/workspaces/{id}/recipes/` typed as `Recipe[]`. Backend apps/recipes/api/views.py:39 serializes that route with `RecipeListSerializer(recipes, many=True)`, whose fields (apps/recipes/api/serializers.py:19-29) OMIT both `prompt` and `variables`. So list items violate the TS type. Reachable on the live recipes route.

2. created_by_name returned, never typed/rendered. RecipeListSerializer (serializers.py:26) and RecipeDetailSerializer (serializers.py:59) both emit `created_by_name`. It is absent from the TS `Recipe` interface and `grep` finds zero references in frontend/src. Confirmed dead-on-arrival field.

3. ArtifactPanel QueryResult.sql required, error entries omit sql. frontend/src/components/ArtifactPanel/ArtifactPanel.tsx:6-14 marks `sql: string` REQUIRED, `error?` optional. Backend apps/artifacts/views.py:804 and :831 build error entries as `{"name": ..., "error": ...}` with NO `sql` key. ArtifactPanel.tsx:280 renders `{query.sql}` unconditionally inside the SQL <pre>; for an error entry `query.sql` is undefined → React renders nothing (empty pre), no crash. Matches the finding's 'none break today but trap the next consumer.'

4. workspaceApi.create narrower than WorkspaceListItem. workspaces.ts:114-118 `create` returns `{id, name}`; WorkspaceListItem (workspaces.ts:16-27) requires `schema_status`+`last_synced_at`, which the backend list endpoint genuinely supplies (apps/workspaces/api/workspace_views.py:158-159). The only consumer, CreateWorkspaceModal.tsx:154-158, uses only `workspace.id` then calls fetchDomains() — so the omission is harmless today.

5. Prompt cache key omits schema/knowledge state; per-process; 60s staleness. apps/agents/graph/base.py:131-142 `_system_prompt_cache_key` keys on `workspace.id : user.id : md5(system_prompt)[:8]` only — no schema state, no materialization timestamp, no knowledge entries. `_build_system_prompt` (base.py:717-722) returns the cached value BEFORE calling `_fetch_schema_context`/`_fetch_multi_tenant_schema_context` (base.py:758/769), and `_system_prompt_cache` (base.py:127) is a module-level dict (per-process), TTL=60 (base.py:128). So a freshly-materialized workspace can serve a stale 'No data has been loaded yet' prompt for up to 60s in that API process. Confirmed exactly as described.

6. runner list-repr. apps/recipes/services/runner.py:164 does `return str(msg.content)` — for content-block list payloads this yields a Python list-repr. Confirmed verbatim.

7. get_schema_status third legacy spelling. mcp_server/server.py:651-652 `get_schema_status` is a registered `@mcp.tool()` and is in MCP_TOOL_NAMES (base.py:72), so it is a live agent tool. server.py:719-724 has the third shape: `elif "table" in result_data and "rows_loaded" in result_data: tables=[{"name": result_data["table"], "row_count": result_data["rows_loaded"]}]` — distinct from the `tables` and `{name,row_count}` spellings.

8. Live SSE empty tool-input. apps/chat/stream.py:190-197 emits `tool-input-available` with `"input": {}` (empty), while the module docstring at stream.py:19 advertises `"input":{...}`. On the live chat-stream path the streamed tool input is empty (real args appear only on checkpoint reload).

Classification COSMETIC / velocity / accidental is appropriate: every item is a type-contract seam or a bounded self-healing staleness window; none break runtime behavior today.
votes.notes: One minor severity nuance, not enough to downgrade the verdict: item 5 (prompt-cache staleness) is slightly more than pure cosmetic — for up to 60s after materialization completes the agent can tell a user 'no data loaded' in the API process. It self-heals within the 60s TTL and the materialization-resume flow re-engages the conversation, so it stays in the velocity/COSMETIC bucket. Tangential (out-of-scope) observation while reading runner.py: its `_build_graph` (runner.py:115) calls `build_agent_graph(tenant_membership=..., user=..., checkpointer=...)`, but base.py:480 `build_agent_graph(workspace, user, checkpointer, mcp_tools, oauth_tokens)` has NO `tenant_membership` kwarg — that call would raise TypeError. That is a separate, more serious defect than anything in this finding and is NOT part of this COSMETIC finding's claims; flagging only so it is not lost. It does not affect this finding's verdict.

title: Wide-open egress + IMDSv1: loader SSRF unbounded, instance-role credential theft reachable
claim: No SecurityGroupEgress is defined on any of the three security groups, so the AWS default (allow all outbound to 0.0.0.0/0, all ports) applies; and the EC2 instance sets no MetadataOptions, so IMDSv1 (HttpTokens=optional) is reachable by a plain GET. A loader that follows a server-supplied next URL can therefore reach the entire internet for exfil, the VPC-internal RDS/Redis, and http://169.254.169.254/latest/meta-data/iam/security-credentials/scout-ec2-role to steal the instance-role credentials. There is zero network-level compensating control behind the missing app-layer URL validation; TODO.md's unchecked 'loader network egress restriction' is confirmed genuinely absent.
chain: infra/scout-stack.yml:96-119 (EC2SecurityGroup ingress-only, default allow-all egress) -> infra/scout-stack.yml:188-232 (no MetadataOptions -> IMDSv1) -> SSRF reach = internet + VPC + IMDS GET returns scout-ec2-role creds

votes.evidence:
I re-read infra/scout-stack.yml in full and independently re-traced every link of the chain.

INFRA FACTS (all confirmed by reading, not trusting quotes):
- `grep -nc 'SecurityGroupEgress|MetadataOptions|HttpTokens|Egress' infra/scout-stack.yml` returns 0 — zero occurrences anywhere in the 476-line template.
- EC2SecurityGroup (lines 96-119) defines only `SecurityGroupIngress` (22/80/443 from 0.0.0.0/0); no egress block. RDSSecurityGroup (121-134) and RedisSecurityGroup (136-149) are also ingress-only. AWS default for a SG with no egress rule = allow ALL outbound to 0.0.0.0/0, all ports. Confirmed.
- EC2Instance (lines 188-232) has InstanceType/ImageId/KeyName/Subnet/SG/IamInstanceProfile/BlockDeviceMappings/UserData but no `MetadataOptions` -> EC2 default `HttpTokens=optional` -> IMDSv1 reachable by a plain GET. Confirmed.
- EC2Role `RoleName: scout-ec2-role` (line 162), referenced by EC2InstanceProfile (153-157) which is attached to the instance (line 197). So `http://169.254.169.254/latest/meta-data/iam/security-credentials/scout-ec2-role` is the exact valid IMDS path. Confirmed.
- RDS (PubliclyAccessible:false, RDSSecurityGroup ingress 5432 from EC2SG, line 126-131) and Redis (RedisSecurityGroup ingress 6379 from EC2SG, 141-146) sit in the same VPC; both accept traffic whose source is the EC2 SG — i.e. any process running ON the instance. So an in-instance SSRF reaches RDS:5432 and Redis:6379. Confirmed.

APP-LAYER SSRF PRIMITIVE (the thing the missing network controls would compensate for):
- mcp_server/loaders/commcare_base.py:49-59 `_resolve_next_url` does `return urljoin(base_url, next_url)` on a server-supplied `meta.next`; an absolute next URL is returned as-is. `_get` (61-69) is a bare `self._session.get(url, ...)` with no host/IP allow-listing.
- mcp_server/loaders/commcare_cases.py:50-68 loops `while url:` ... `data = self._get(url).json()` ... `url = data.get("next")` — follows the raw server `next` field directly.
- mcp_server/loaders/commcare_forms.py:50-70 same loop via `_resolve_next_url(...data.get("meta",{}).get("next"))`.
- mcp_server/loaders/connect_base.py:156-235 follows the server-built `next` URL until null and (comment lines 187-194) explicitly relies on `requests` default `allow_redirects=True`, even following http->https redirects — an added redirect-based SSRF amplifier.
- mcp_server/loaders/ocs_base.py:45-84 same `current_url = payload.get("next")` follow loop.
None of the loaders validate the host/scheme of the next URL.

REACHABILITY (live path today):
- mcp_server/services/materializer.py:57-72 imports and instantiates all these loaders; line 107 documents the LOAD phase executing loaders and stream-writing to tenant schema.
- mcp_server/server.py:521 exposes `run_materialization` as an MCP tool (plus get/cancel at 408/446); apps/workspaces/tasks.py also drives the materializer as a Procrastinate task. So the SSRF-capable loaders are wired to a live MCP tool and a background task.

TODO confirmation: TODO.md:41 `- [ ] **Network isolation for loaders** — restrict loader subprocess egress to configured API endpoints only` is unchecked — the loader-egress restriction is genuinely absent, matching the finding.

Every infra fact, the SSRF primitive, the absence of any network compensating control, and the live reachability are reproduced. LATENT is the correct status: exploitation requires a poisoned `next` URL from upstream (a compromised/malicious trusted API host or an on-path MITM), since base URLs default to first-party settings, not benign default operation.
votes.notes:
Two precision notes that refine (but do not refute) the finding:

1) Blast radius of the IMDS-stolen creds is moderate, not catastrophic. scout-ec2-role (lines 159-186) grants only AmazonSSMManagedInstanceCore + CloudWatch Logs write (CreateLogStream/PutLogEvents/DescribeLogStreams). The secrets-manager access lives on a SEPARATE role, GitHubDeployRole/scout-github-deploy (400-451), which is assumed via GitHub OIDC and is NOT on the instance profile. So IMDS theft yields SSM-messaging + log-write creds, not Secrets Manager. The finding only claims "instance-role credentials," which is accurate, but a reader should not infer secret-store access from the IMDS path.

2) The SSRF precondition is upstream-controlled. Loader base URLs come from server settings (connect_base.py:109/122 CONNECT_API_URL, ocs_base.py:27/37 OCS_URL) with first-party defaults (connect.dimagi.com, openchatstudio.com), not directly tenant-arbitrary in these classes. So a poisoned `next` requires a compromised/malicious trusted upstream or MITM — which is exactly why LATENT (not BROKEN-NOW) is the right status. connect_base.py:187-194's reliance on allow_redirects=True (including http->https) makes redirect-based injection easier and is worth fixing alongside.

3) Minor wording: TODO/finding say "subprocess" egress, but the loaders actually run in-process inside materializer.py (direct imports, requests.Session). This does not change reachability — allow-all egress + IMDSv1 affect any process on the instance equally.

Recommended remediation pairs cleanly: (a) add SecurityGroupEgress restricting outbound to the needed API endpoints/443 + DNS; (b) add MetadataOptions HttpTokens=required (IMDSv2) and HttpPutResponseHopLimit=1; (c) add app-layer next-URL host/scheme allow-listing and allow_redirects=False in the loaders.

votes.evidence:
I independently re-read infra/scout-stack.yml and the loader code; every material claim holds.

EGRESS: The three security groups (scout-stack.yml:96-119 EC2SecurityGroup, :121-134 RDSSecurityGroup, :136-149 RedisSecurityGroup) each declare ONLY `SecurityGroupIngress`. No `SecurityGroupEgress` anywhere in the file (grep confirmed zero matches). CloudFormation leaves the AWS default allow-all-outbound rule (0.0.0.0/0, all ports) in place when egress is omitted. The template also defines no `AWS::EC2::NetworkAcl`, so the default NACL (allow-all) applies — there is genuinely zero network-level egress control.

IMDS: EC2Instance (scout-stack.yml:188-232) has no `MetadataOptions` property -> HttpTokens defaults to `optional`, so IMDSv1 is reachable by a plain GET. The instance launches into `SubnetId: !Ref PublicSubnetA` (line 194), which has `MapPublicIpOnLaunch: true` (line 53) and a 0.0.0.0/0 route to the IGW (PublicRoute, lines 74-80), plus an ElasticIP (lines 234-237) — confirming wide-open internet egress. The role `scout-ec2-role` exists (EC2Role RoleName line 162) and is wired via EC2InstanceProfile (lines 153-157), so GET http://169.254.169.254/latest/meta-data/iam/security-credentials/scout-ec2-role returns live STS creds.

SSRF SURFACE (app layer, runs on this EC2 via the MCP server): loaders follow server-supplied `next` URLs with NO host/scheme validation — connect_base.py:234 `url = payload.get(\"next\")` with allow_redirects=True (lines 187-197); ocs_base.py:84 `current_url = payload.get(\"next\")`; commcare_cases.py:68 `url = data.get(\"next\")`; commcare_base.py:49-59 `_resolve_next_url` is a bare `urljoin(base_url, next_url)`. No allowlist anywhere. These loaders are invoked by live materialization tasks (apps/workspaces/tasks.py), so the path is reachable today.

TODO.md:41 `[ ] Network isolation for loaders — restrict loader subprocess egress to configured API endpoints only` is unchecked — the compensating control is genuinely absent.
votes.notes: Two refinements that do NOT change the verdict (finding does not overclaim either): (1) The stolen scout-ec2-role creds carry only AmazonSSMManagedInstanceCore + CloudWatch-logs perms (EC2Role lines 170-186). The Secrets Manager/RDS-secret access lives on the SEPARATE GitHubDeployRole (lines 400-451), which is OIDC-assumed by GitHub Actions and NOT reachable via IMDS — so immediate blast radius of instance-role theft is modest (SSM session + log write), though the SSRF still independently reaches internet exfil and VPC-internal RDS:5432 / Redis:6379. (2) IMDSv1-reachability assumes the account has not set an account-level IMDSv2-required default and the Canonical Ubuntu 24.04 AMI is not registered with imds-support=v2.0 (which would force IMDSv2 regardless); the egress/internet/VPC portions of the finding hold unconditionally either way. LATENT is the correct status: exploitation requires an attacker to control an upstream `next`/redirect response (most plausibly via the configurable OCS base_url or a compromised/MITM'd upstream), not a directly user-driven request today.

title: CI deploy role over-scoped: account-wide secret prefix wildcards read every RDS master password
claim: GitHubDeployRole's secretsmanager:GetSecretValue is scoped by account-wide prefix wildcards, not specific ARNs: rds!* matches every RDS-managed master-password secret in the account (not just scout-db), and SCOUT_*/COMMCARE_*/CONNECT_*/OCS_* match any same-prefixed secret regardless of owner. It also grants BatchGetSecretValue on Resource '*'. Trust is correctly pinned to repo:dimagi-rad/scout:ref:refs/heads/main, but within a CI run the role reads more than it needs.
chain: infra/scout-stack.yml:438-447 (GetSecretValue Resource includes secret:rds!* and four secret:<PREFIX>_* wildcards) -> infra/scout-stack.yml:448-451 (BatchGetSecretValue on Resource '*')

votes.evidence:
Re-traced independently from template to live CI path:

1. Policy wildcards — /Users/bderenzi/Code/dimagi/scout/infra/scout-stack.yml:438-447: the `scout-deploy-policy` on `GitHubDeployRole` (RoleName `scout-github-deploy`, line 403) grants `secretsmanager:GetSecretValue` on:
   - line 442: `secret:scout/*`
   - line 443: `secret:rds!*`
   - lines 444-447: `secret:SCOUT_*`, `secret:COMMCARE_*`, `secret:CONNECT_*`, `secret:OCS_*`
   All are `${AWS::Region}:${AWS::AccountId}` prefix wildcards, no owner/tag condition. RDS-managed master-user secrets are auto-named `rds!db-<uuid>`, so `rds!*` matches every RDS-managed master password in the account+region, not just scout-db. The prefix wildcards likewise match any same-prefixed secret regardless of which app owns it.

2. BatchGetSecretValue — lines 448-451: `secretsmanager:BatchGetSecretValue` on `Resource: '*'`, exactly as claimed.

3. Trust pinning — lines 411-415: `StringLike token.actions.githubusercontent.com:sub: repo:dimagi-rad/scout:ref:refs/heads/main` plus `aud: sts.amazonaws.com`. Correctly pinned to main of one repo, as the finding states.

4. Reachability (live path): /Users/bderenzi/Code/dimagi/scout/.github/workflows/deploy.yml:3-5 triggers on every push to main; line 30 assumes the role via `role-to-assume: ${{ secrets.SCOUT_GITHUB_DEPLOY_ROLE_ARN }}`. The Kamal deploys (lines 73-83) read secrets through /Users/bderenzi/Code/dimagi/scout/.kamal/secrets, which runs `kamal secrets fetch --adapter aws_secrets_manager` for COMMCARE_*/CONNECT_*/OCS_*/SCOUT_* names (this adapter uses batch-get-secret-value, explaining the '*' grant) and /Users/bderenzi/Code/dimagi/scout/scripts/resolve-database-url.sh fetches the RDS secret via `aws secretsmanager get-secret-value --secret-id "$SCOUT_RDS_SECRET_ARN"` — i.e. CI already uses the exact ARN, proving `rds!*` is broader than the deploy actually needs.

5. Least-privilege alternative exists in-template: the exact secret ARN is available as `!GetAtt RDSInstance.MasterUserSecret.SecretArn` (used in Outputs at scout-stack.yml:462-464), so the policy could reference it directly instead of `rds!*`.
votes.notes: Core claim fully reproduced; status LATENT and impact security are correct (exploitation requires code execution in a main-branch CI run — pushed/merged code or a compromised third-party action like ruby/setup-ruby, webfactory/ssh-agent, etc.). Two qualifications: (1) The BatchGetSecretValue-on-'*' item is factually present but effectively benign on its own — AWS requires per-secret GetSecretValue in addition to BatchGetSecretValue, and Resource '*' on the batch action is AWS's documented pattern (it is needed because Kamal's aws_secrets_manager adapter calls batch-get-secret-value). The real over-grant is entirely in the GetSecretValue resource list. (2) Actual blast radius of `rds!*` depends on whether other RDS instances with ManageMasterUserPassword share this account+region — not determinable from the repo; in a shared Dimagi account the COMMCARE_* wildcard is also plausibly cross-app. Fix is trivial: use `!GetAtt RDSInstance.MasterUserSecret.SecretArn` for the RDS secret and exact-name-plus-`-??????` suffix patterns for the named secrets.

votes.evidence:
Re-traced independently from the workflow entry point to the IAM grant.

1. Policy text (infra/scout-stack.yml:438-447): the GitHubDeployRole inline policy grants secretsmanager:GetSecretValue on six name-prefix wildcard ARNs, including `!Sub 'arn:aws:secretsmanager:${AWS::Region}:${AWS::AccountId}:secret:rds!*'` (line 443) and `secret:SCOUT_*` / `secret:COMMCARE_*` / `secret:CONNECT_*` / `secret:OCS_*` (lines 444-447), plus `secret:scout/*` (line 442). RDS-managed master-user secrets are named `rds!db-<uuid>`, so `secret:rds!*` matches EVERY RDS-managed master password in the account+region, not just scout-db. The prefix wildcards likewise match any same-prefixed secret regardless of owner.

2. The over-scope is avoidable in-template: Outputs at infra/scout-stack.yml:462-464 already reference `!GetAtt RDSInstance.MasterUserSecret.SecretArn`, proving the exact secret ARN is resolvable in the same template and could be used in the policy instead of `rds!*`.

3. BatchGetSecretValue on Resource '*' confirmed at infra/scout-stack.yml:448-451.

4. Trust policy confirmed pinned at infra/scout-stack.yml:414-415: `StringLike: token.actions.githubusercontent.com:sub: repo:dimagi-rad/scout:ref:refs/heads/main` with `aud: sts.amazonaws.com` (line 413).

5. Reachability — live on every main push: .github/workflows/deploy.yml:3-5 triggers on push to main; line 30 assumes the role via `role-to-assume: ${{ secrets.SCOUT_GITHUB_DEPLOY_ROLE_ARN }}`. The role's secret permissions are actively exercised: .kamal/secrets runs `kamal secrets fetch --adapter aws_secrets_manager ...` (which uses BatchGetSecretValue) for the COMMCARE_/CONNECT_/OCS_/SCOUT_ secrets, and scripts/resolve-database-url.sh calls `aws secretsmanager get-secret-value --secret-id "$SCOUT_RDS_SECRET_ARN"` for the RDS master password during kamal deploys (deploy.yml:73-83).
votes.notes: One mechanism nuance that does not change the verdict: BatchGetSecretValue on Resource '*' is the AWS-documented pattern and is not itself an independent over-grant — per AWS semantics, BatchGetSecretValue additionally requires secretsmanager:GetSecretValue on each individual secret retrieved, so effective read access remains bounded by the GetSecretValue statement (lines 438-447). The real over-scope is entirely in the GetSecretValue prefix wildcards, with `rds!*` being the worst (every RDS master password in the account) and the most clearly fixable (exact ARN available via `!GetAtt RDSInstance.MasterUserSecret.SecretArn`, as the template's own Outputs demonstrate). Note that scoping the four name-prefix env-var secrets exactly still requires a trailing `-??????` wildcard for the random ARN suffix (e.g. `secret:SCOUT_DJANGO_SECRET_KEY-??????`), which is the standard tight form. LATENT is the right status: exploitation requires compromise of main-branch CI (or a merged malicious workflow change), at which point blast radius extends to all same-account RDS instances and same-prefixed secrets owned by other stacks.
